title: The Insurance Desk
subtitle: What a probability actually means
realWorldHook: You run a small-town car-insurance company. You can't predict who crashes — but watch what 'unpredictable' does to your bank account as the business grows.
conceptSummary: Probability is long-run relative frequency: a single crash is unpredictable, but the crash rate is rock-steady at scale.

scenario.role: You run a small-town car-insurance company.

steps.title: Here's your town
steps.body:
Ten drivers. Each pays you a premium to insure their car. Some year, one of them will crash and you'll write a big check.

You *can't* predict who — randomness is lumpy up close. So the whole company rides on a quiet bet: that the crowd, taken together, is far steadier than any single driver.

That tension — unsure about any one driver, betting the whole company on the crowd — is what probability is really about. In a moment you'll sit at the desk and run it yourself.

steps.prompt: You charge each driver **$800**. A crash costs you a $12,000 payout, and about 5% of drivers crash each year — so your *expected* claim is only ~**$600** per driver. With just 10 customers, will you turn a profit *every single year*?

options.label: Yes — $800 in, $600 expected out. The margin's positive, so I win every year.
Most years, but the odd bad year could sting.
No — with only 10 drivers the swings could wipe me out some years.

steps.revealMessage: Let's stop arguing and actually run your company for a few years.

revealByOption.yes: That's the gut answer — positive margin *feels* like guaranteed profit. Run the desk and watch a single crash blow up a 10-driver year.
revealByOption.mostly: Warmer — you sense the wobble. Let's see exactly how big a 'sting' a tiny book of business can take.
revealByOption.no: Sharp instinct. Few customers means wild swings. Let's prove it, then watch the swings vanish at scale.

steps.prompt: Start at 10 drivers and hit **Run year** a few times — feel the bankroll lurch when someone crashes. Then **grow the business** to thousands of drivers and run again. Watch how the swings behave at scale.
At 10 customers you booked a loss in some years even though every premium was profitable on average. At 5,000 your bankroll barely budged. Why?

options.label: Bigger companies somehow make drivers crash less often.
Small samples are noisy; the average crash rate only shows up at scale.
After a few crashes the others become 'due' not to crash, so it evens out.

feedback.correct: Right. Your premium was always profitable on average — but with 10 drivers, one bad year of crashes swamps the average. Scale is what turns a 'risky bet' into a stable business.
feedback.incorrect: Think back to how your bankroll swung — you booked a loss in some years even though the math 'should' win. Few customers means wild swings; the average only rescues you at scale.

byOption.a: The crash rate is exactly 5% the whole time — your customers don't drive differently. What changes is how much the *average* of many drivers wobbles year to year.
byOption.c: That's the [[gambler's fallacy|The mistaken belief that past random outcomes change future ones — that a crash makes others less likely, as if to 'balance out'. Each driver's risk is unchanged; only the average steadies with more of them.]]. Nobody is 'due'. The numbers steady only because you're averaging over more drivers.

steps.hint: Remember how much your bankroll jumped at 10 drivers versus 5,000. What's actually shrinking — the risk per driver, or the wobble in the average?

steps.prompt: Your margin is **+$200 per driver on average**. With just 10 drivers, what does that *guarantee* for next year?

options.label: A profit of about $2,000 — 10 drivers × $200 each.
Nothing certain — it's a long-run average, and one crash can still post a loss.

feedback.correct: Exactly. '+$200 on average' is the long-run trend, not a promise. With 10 drivers a single $12,000 payout dwarfs $8,000 of premiums — a losing year, even on a winning margin.
feedback.incorrect: That's the *average*, not a guarantee. One crash is a $12,000 hit against $8,000 of premiums — a loss that year. The +$200 only reliably shows up across thousands of drivers.

byOption.a: That's the *expected* profit, but a single $12,000 crash against 10 premiums ($8,000) is a losing year. The average only becomes reliable at scale.

steps.hint: Expected value is a long-run average. How many $800 premiums does one $12,000 payout eat up?

steps.title: The long run, in one line
steps.body:
A probability is a [[long-run relative frequency|The fraction of times an outcome happens if you repeat the situation a *huge* number of times. That settled fraction is exactly what we call its probability.]] — what the crash rate settles to over many, many drivers.

Your $200 [[expected value|The long-run average result per driver: premium minus the average claim, here $800 − $600 = $200. It's what you earn *on average*, never a promise for one driver or one year.]] margin per driver is real — but it only *rescues you* once you have enough drivers for the average to show up. That's the [[law of large numbers|The more drivers (or trials) you average over, the closer the result lands to the true probability. Small books of business swing wildly; large ones barely budge.]].

It's the same math a casino runs: it can't predict one spin, but it banks on the month. Next, you'll start combining chances together.
